refers to the fourth generation of [cellular network](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network") technology, introduced in the late 2000s and early 2010s. Compared to preceding third-generation ([3G](/wiki/3G "3G")) technologies, 4G has been designed to support [all-IP](/wiki/All-IP "All-IP") communications and [broadband](/wiki/Mobile_broadband "Mobile broadband") services, and eliminates [circuit switching](/wiki/Circuit_switching "Circuit switching") in voice telephony.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) It also has considerably higher data bandwidth compared to 3G, enabling a variety of data-intensive applications[[2]](#cite_note-2) such as [high-definition](/wiki/HDTV "HDTV") [media streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") and the expansion of [Internet of Things](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things") (IoT) applications.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)

Broadband cellular network technology

**4G** refers to the fourth generation of [cellular network](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network") technology, introduced in the late 2000s and early 2010s. Compared to preceding third-generation ([3G](/wiki/3G "3G")) technologies, 4G has been designed to support [all-IP](/wiki/All-IP "All-IP") communications and [broadband](/wiki/Mobile_broadband "Mobile broadband") services, and eliminates [circuit switching](/wiki/Circuit_switching "Circuit switching") in voice telephony.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) It also has considerably higher data bandwidth compared to 3G, enabling a variety of data-intensive applications[[2]](#cite_note-2) such as [high-definition](/wiki/HDTV "HDTV") [media streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") and the expansion of [Internet of Things](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things") (IoT) applications.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)

The earliest deployed technologies marketed as "4G" were [Long Term Evolution](/wiki/LTE_(telecommunication) "LTE (telecommunication)") (LTE), developed by the [3GPP](/wiki/3GPP "3GPP") group, and [Mobile Worldwide Interoperability for Microwave Access](/wiki/WiMAX "WiMAX") (Mobile WiMAX), based on [IEEE](/wiki/IEEE "IEEE") specifications.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) These provided significant enhancements over previous 3G and [2G](/wiki/2G "2G").

## Technical overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Technical overview")]

In November 2008, the [International Telecommunication Union-Radio communications sector](/wiki/ITU-R "ITU-R") (ITU-R) specified a set of requirements for 4G standards, named the International Mobile Telecommunications Advanced (IMT-Advanced) specification, setting peak speed requirements for 4G service at 100 [megabits per second](/wiki/Megabits_per_second "Megabits per second") (Mbit/s)(=12.5 megabytes per second) for high mobility communication (such as from trains and cars) and 1 [gigabit per second](/wiki/Gigabit_per_second "Gigabit per second") (Gbit/s) for low mobility communication (such as pedestrians and stationary users).[[5]](#cite_note-IMT-Advanced-requirements-5)

Since the first-release versions of [Mobile WiMAX](/wiki/Mobile_WiMAX "Mobile WiMAX") and [LTE](/wiki/Long_Term_Evolution "Long Term Evolution") support much less than 1 Gbit/s peak bit rate, they are not fully IMT-Advanced compliant, but are often branded 4G by service providers. According to operators, a generation of the network refers to the deployment of a new non-backward-compatible technology. On December 6, 2010, ITU-R recognized that these two technologies, as well as other beyond-3G technologies that do not fulfill the IMT-Advanced requirements, could nevertheless be considered "4G", provided they represent forerunners to IMT-Advanced compliant versions and "a substantial level of improvement in performance and capabilities with respect to the initial third generation systems now deployed". Both the original LTE and WiMAX standards had previously sometimes been referred to as 3.9G/3.95G.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) The ITU's new definition for 4G also included [Evolved High Speed Packet Access](/wiki/Evolved_High_Speed_Packet_Access "Evolved High Speed Packet Access") (HSPA+).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[Mobile WiMAX Release 2](/wiki/Mobile_WiMAX_Release_2 "Mobile WiMAX Release 2") (also known as *WirelessMAN-Advanced* or *IEEE 802.16m*) and [LTE Advanced](/wiki/LTE_Advanced "LTE Advanced")
(LTE-A) are IMT-Advanced compliant backwards compatible versions of the above two systems, standardized during the spring 2011,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and promising speeds in the order of 1 Gbit/s. In January 2012, the ITU backtracked on its previous definition for 4G, claiming that Mobile WiMAX 2 and LTE Advanced are "true 4G" while their predecessors are "transitional" 3G-4G.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)

As opposed to earlier generations, a 4G system does not support traditional [circuit-switched](/wiki/Circuit-switched "Circuit-switched") telephony service, but instead relies on all-[Internet Protocol](/wiki/Internet_Protocol "Internet Protocol") (IP) based communication such as [IP telephony](/wiki/IP_telephony "IP telephony"). As seen below, the [spread spectrum](/wiki/Spread_spectrum "Spread spectrum") radio technology used in 3G systems is abandoned in all 4G candidate systems and replaced by [OFDMA](/wiki/OFDMA "OFDMA") [multi-carrier](/wiki/Multi-carrier "Multi-carrier") transmission and other [frequency-domain equalization](/wiki/Single-carrier_FDMA "Single-carrier FDMA") (FDE) schemes, making it possible to transfer very high bit rates despite extensive [multi-path radio propagation](/wiki/Multipath_propagation "Multipath propagation") (echoes). The peak bit rate is further improved by [smart antenna](/wiki/Smart_antenna "Smart antenna") arrays for [multiple-input multiple-output](/wiki/MIMO "MIMO") (MIMO) communications.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

In the field of mobile communications, a "generation" generally refers to a change in the fundamental nature of the service, non-backwards-compatible transmission technology, higher peak bit rates, new frequency bands, wider channel frequency bandwidth in Hertz, and higher capacity for many simultaneous data transfers (higher [system spectral efficiency](/wiki/System_spectral_efficiency "System spectral efficiency") in [bit](/wiki/Bit "Bit")/second/Hertz/site).

New mobile generations have appeared about every ten years since the first move from 1981 analog (1G) to digital (2G) transmission in 1992. This was followed, in 2001, by 3G multi-media support, [spread spectrum](/wiki/Spread_spectrum "Spread spectrum") transmission and a minimum peak bit rate of 200 [kbit/s](/wiki/Kbit/s "Kbit/s"), in 2011/2012 to be followed by "real" 4G, which refers to all-IP [packet-switched](/wiki/Packet_switching "Packet switching") networks giving mobile ultra-broadband (gigabit speed) access.

While the ITU has adopted recommendations for technologies that would be used for future global communications, they do not actually perform the standardization or development work themselves, instead relying on the work of other standard bodies such as IEEE, WiMAX Forum, and 3GPP.

In the mid-1990s, the [ITU-R](/wiki/ITU-R "ITU-R") standardization organization released the [IMT-2000](/wiki/IMT-2000 "IMT-2000") requirements as a framework for what standards should be considered [3G](/wiki/3G "3G") systems, requiring 2000 kbit/s peak bit rate.[[9]](#cite_note-:65-9) The fastest 3G-based standard in the [UMTS](/wiki/UMTS "UMTS") family is the [HSPA+](/wiki/HSPA%2B "HSPA+") standard, which has been commercially available since 2009 and offers 21 Mbit/s downstream (11 Mbit/s upstream) without [MIMO](/wiki/MIMO "MIMO"), i.e. with only one antenna, and in 2011 accelerated up to 42 Mbit/s peak bit rate downstream using either [DC-HSPA+](/wiki/Dual-Cell_HSDPA "Dual-Cell HSDPA") (simultaneous use of two 5 MHz UMTS carriers)[[10]](#cite_note-LteWorld-10) or
2x2 MIMO. In theory speeds up to 672 Mbit/s are possible, but have not been deployed yet. The fastest 3G-based standard in the [CDMA2000](/wiki/CDMA2000 "CDMA2000") family is the [EV-DO Rev. B](/wiki/EV-DO_Rev._B "EV-DO Rev. B"), which is available since 2010 and offers 15.67 Mbit/s downstream.

In 2008, ITU-R specified the [IMT Advanced](/wiki/IMT_Advanced "IMT Advanced") (International Mobile Telecommunications Advanced) requirements for 4G systems.

## IMT-Advanced requirements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: IMT-Advanced requirements")]

This article refers to 4G using IMT-Advanced (*International Mobile Telecommunications Advanced*), as defined by [ITU-R](/wiki/ITU-R "ITU-R"). An IMT-Advanced [cellular system](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") must fulfill the following requirements:[[11]](#cite_note-Vilches,_J._2010-11)

* Be based on an all-IP packet switched network.
* Have peak data rates of up to approximately 100 Mbit/s for high mobility such as mobile access and up to approximately 1 Gbit/s for low mobility such as nomadic/local wireless access.[[5]](#cite_note-IMT-Advanced-requirements-5)
* Be able to dynamically share and use the network resources to support more simultaneous users per cell.
* Use scalable channel bandwidths of 5–20 MHz, optionally up to 40 MHz.[[5]](#cite_note-IMT-Advanced-requirements-5)[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* Have peak [link spectral efficiency](/wiki/Link_spectral_efficiency "Link spectral efficiency") of 15 bit/s·Hz in the downlink, and 6.75 bit/s·Hz in the up link (meaning that 1 Gbit/s in the downlink should be possible over less than 67 MHz bandwidth).
* [System spectral efficiency](/wiki/System_spectral_efficiency "System spectral efficiency") is, in indoor cases, 3 bit/s·Hz·cell for downlink and 2.25 bit/s·Hz·cell for up link.[[5]](#cite_note-IMT-Advanced-requirements-5)
* Smooth handovers across heterogeneous networks.

In September 2009, the technology proposals were submitted to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) as 4G candidates.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Basically all proposals are based on two technologies:

* [LTE Advanced](/wiki/LTE_Advanced "LTE Advanced") standardized by the [3GPP](/wiki/3GPP "3GPP")
* [802.16m](/wiki/802.16m "802.16m") standardized by the [IEEE](/wiki/IEEE "IEEE")

Implementations of Mobile WiMAX and first-release LTE were largely considered a stopgap solution that would offer a considerable boost until WiMAX 2 (based on the 802.16m specification) and LTE Advanced was deployed. The latter's standard versions were ratified in spring 2011.

The first set of 3GPP requirements on LTE Advanced was approved in June 2008.[[14]](#cite_note-14) LTE Advanced was standardized in 2010 as part of Release 10 of the 3GPP specification.

Some sources consider first-release LTE and Mobile WiMAX implementations as pre-4G or near-4G, as they do not fully comply with the planned requirements of 1 Gbit/s for stationary reception and 100 Mbit/s for mobile.

Confusion has been caused by some mobile carriers who have launched products advertised as 4G but which according to some sources are pre-4G versions, commonly referred to as 3.9G, which do not follow the ITU-R defined principles for 4G standards, but today can be called 4G according to ITU-R. [Vodafone Netherlands](/wiki/Vodafone_Netherlands "Vodafone Netherlands") for example, advertised LTE as 4G, while advertising LTE Advanced as their '4G+' service. A common argument for branding 3.9G systems as new-generation is that they use different frequency bands from 3G technologies; that they are based on a new radio-interface paradigm; and that the standards are not backwards compatible with 3G, whilst some of the standards are forwards compatible with IMT-2000 compliant versions of the same standards.

## System standards

### IMT-2000 compliant 4G standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: IMT-2000 compliant 4G standards")]

As of October 2010, ITU-R Working Party 5D approved two industry-developed technologies (LTE Advanced and WirelessMAN-Advanced)[[15]](#cite_note-ITU_paves_way_for_next-generation_4G_mobile_technologies-15) for inclusion in the ITU's International Mobile Telecommunications Advanced program ([IMT-Advanced](/wiki/IMT-Advanced "IMT-Advanced") program), which is focused on global communication systems that will be available several years from now.

#### LTE Advanced

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: LTE Advanced")]

Main article: [LTE Advanced](/wiki/LTE_Advanced "LTE Advanced")

[LTE Advanced](/wiki/LTE_Advanced "LTE Advanced") (Long Term Evolution Advanced) is a candidate for [IMT-Advanced](/wiki/IMT-Advanced "IMT-Advanced") standard, formally submitted by the [3GPP](/wiki/3GPP "3GPP") organization to ITU-T in the fall 2009, and as of 2013 has been released to the public.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]The target of 3GPP LTE Advanced is to reach and surpass the ITU requirements.[[17]](#cite_note-17) LTE Advanced is essentially an enhancement to LTE. It is not a new technology, but rather an improvement on the existing LTE network. This upgrade path makes it more cost effective for vendors to offer LTE and then upgrade to LTE Advanced which is similar to the upgrade from WCDMA to HSPA. LTE and LTE Advanced will also make use of additional spectrums and multiplexing to allow it to achieve higher data speeds. Coordinated Multi-point Transmission will also allow more system capacity to help handle the enhanced data speeds.

#### Long Term Evolution (LTE)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Long Term Evolution (LTE)")]

Main article: [LTE (telecommunication)](/wiki/LTE_(telecommunication) "LTE (telecommunication)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Samsung_4G_LTE_modem-4.jpg/250px-Samsung_4G_LTE_modem-4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samsung_4G_LTE_modem-4.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Huawei_4G%2B_Modem.jpg/250px-Huawei_4G%2B_Modem.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huawei_4G%2B_Modem.jpg)

Huawei 4G+ Dual Band Modem

The pre-4G [3GPP Long Term Evolution](/wiki/3GPP_Long_Term_Evolution "3GPP Long Term Evolution") (LTE) technology is often branded "4G – LTE", but the first LTE release does not fully comply with the IMT-Advanced requirements. LTE has a theoretical [net bit rate](/wiki/Net_bit_rate "Net bit rate") capacity of up to 100 Mbit/s in the downlink and 50 Mbit/s in the uplink if a 20 MHz channel is used — and more if [multiple-input multiple-output](/wiki/Multiple-input_multiple-output "Multiple-input multiple-output") (MIMO), i.e. antenna arrays, are used.

The physical radio interface was at an early stage named *High Speed [OFDM](/wiki/OFDM "OFDM") Packet Access* (HSOPA), now named [Evolved UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access](/wiki/Evolved_UMTS_Terrestrial_Radio_Access "Evolved UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access") (E-UTRA).
The first [LTE](/wiki/LTE_(telecommunication) "LTE (telecommunication)") USB dongles do not support any other radio interface.

The world's first publicly available LTE service was opened in the two Scandinavian capitals, [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") ([Ericsson](/wiki/Ericsson "Ericsson") and [Nokia Siemens Networks](/wiki/Nokia_Solutions_and_Networks "Nokia Solutions and Networks") systems) and [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") (a [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei") system) on December 14, 2009, and branded 4G. The user terminals were manufactured by Samsung.[[19]](#cite_note-deepak_kirdoliya-19) As of November 2012, the five publicly available LTE services in the United States are provided by [MetroPCS](/wiki/MetroPCS "MetroPCS"),[[20]](#cite_note-MetroPCS-20) [Verizon Wireless](/wiki/Verizon_Wireless "Verizon Wireless"),[[21]](#cite_note-VerizonLTE-21) [AT&T Mobility](/wiki/AT%26T_Mobility "AT&T Mobility"), [U.S. Cellular](/wiki/U.S._Cellular "U.S. Cellular"),[[22]](#cite_note-USCellular-22) [Sprint](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation"),[[23]](#cite_note-SprintTE-23) and [T-Mobile US](/wiki/T-Mobile_US "T-Mobile US").[[24]](#cite_note-T-MobileLTE-24)

T-Mobile Hungary launched a public beta test (called *friendly user test*) on 7 October 2011, and has offered commercial 4G LTE services since 1 January 2012.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In South Korea, SK Telecom and LG U+ have enabled access to LTE service since 1 July 2011 for data devices, slated to go nationwide by 2012.[[25]](#cite_note-25) KT Telecom closed its 2G service by March 2012 and completed nationwide LTE service in the same frequency around 1.8 GHz by June 2012.

In the United Kingdom, LTE services were launched by [EE](/wiki/EE_(telecommunications) "EE (telecommunications)") in October 2012,[[26]](#cite_note-26) by [O2](/wiki/O2_(United_Kingdom) "O2 (United Kingdom)") and [Vodafone](/wiki/Vodafone_UK "Vodafone UK") in August 2013,[[27]](#cite_note-27) and by [Three](/wiki/Three_UK "Three UK") in December 2013.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The [Mobile WiMAX](/wiki/Mobile_WiMAX "Mobile WiMAX") (IEEE 802.16e-2005) mobile wireless broadband access (MWBA) standard (also known as [WiBro](/wiki/WiBro "WiBro") in South Korea) is sometimes branded 4G, and offers peak data rates of 128 Mbit/s downlink and 56 Mbit/s uplink over 20 MHz wide channels. As [Long-Term Evolution](/wiki/3GPP_Long_Term_Evolution "3GPP Long Term Evolution") (LTE) and WiMAX were adopted by the global telecommunications industry, LTE emerged as a primary 4G standard and saw significant adoption in the Chinese market. Although [TD-LTE](/wiki/TD-LTE "TD-LTE"), one of the two LTE air interface variants, was initially less mature than its FDD-LTE counterpart, various domestic and international wireless carriers eventually adopted the technology..

IBM's data shows that 67% of the operators are considering LTE because this is the main source of their future market. The above news also confirms IBM's statement that while only 8% of the operators are considering the use of WiMAX, WiMAX can provide the fastest network transmission to its customers on the market and could challenge LTE.

TD-LTE is not the first 4G wireless mobile broadband network data standard, but it is China's 4G standard that was amended and published by China's largest telecom operator – [China Mobile](/wiki/China_Mobile "China Mobile"). After a series of field trials, is expected to be released into the commercial phase in the next two years. Ulf Ewaldsson, Ericsson's vice president said: "the Chinese Ministry of Industry and China Mobile in the fourth quarter of this year will hold a large-scale field test, by then, Ericsson will help the hand." But viewing from the current development trend, whether this standard advocated by China Mobile will be widely recognized by the international market is still debatable.

### Key features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Key features")]

The following key features can be observed in all suggested 4G technologies:

* Physical layer transmission techniques are as follows:[[34]](#cite_note-WWRF_WG5-34)
  + [MIMO](/wiki/MIMO "MIMO"): To attain ultra high spectral efficiency by means of spatial processing including multi-antenna and multi-user MIMO
  + Frequency-domain-equalization, for example *multi-carrier modulation* ([OFDM](/wiki/OFDM "OFDM")) in the downlink or *single-carrier frequency-domain-equalization* (SC-FDE) in the uplink: To exploit the frequency selective channel property without complex equalization
  + Frequency-domain statistical multiplexing, for example ([OFDMA](/wiki/OFDMA "OFDMA")) or (single-carrier FDMA) (SC-FDMA, a.k.a. linearly precoded OFDMA, LP-OFDMA) in the uplink: Variable bit rate by assigning different sub-channels to different users based on the channel conditions.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
  + [Turbo principle](/wiki/Turbo_code "Turbo code") [error-correcting codes](/wiki/Error-correcting_code "Error-correcting code"): To minimize the required [SNR](/wiki/Signal-to-noise_ratio "Signal-to-noise ratio") at the reception side
* [Channel-dependent scheduling](/wiki/Channel-dependent_scheduling "Channel-dependent scheduling"): To use the time-varying channel
* [Link adaptation](/wiki/Link_adaptation "Link adaptation"): [Adaptive modulation](/wiki/Adaptive_modulation "Adaptive modulation") and error-correcting codes
* [Mobile IP](/wiki/Mobile_IP "Mobile IP") utilized for mobility
* IP-based [femtocells](/wiki/Femtocell "Femtocell") (home nodes connected to fixed Internet broadband infrastructure)

As opposed to earlier generations, 4G systems do not support circuit switched telephony. IEEE 802.20, UMB and OFDM standards[[36]](#cite_note-36) lack [soft-handover](/w/index.php?title=Soft-handover&action=edit&redlink=1 "Soft-handover (page does not exist)") support, also known as [cooperative relaying](/wiki/Cooperative_wireless_communications "Cooperative wireless communications").

### Multiplexing and access schemes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Multiplexing and access schemes")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section may contain information **not [important or relevant](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not#Encyclopedic_content "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not") to the article's subject.** Please help [improve this section](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit). *(May 2010)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Recently, new access schemes like [Orthogonal FDMA](/wiki/OFDMA "OFDMA") (OFDMA), [Single Carrier FDMA](/wiki/SC-FDMA "SC-FDMA") (SC-FDMA), Interleaved FDMA, and [Multi-carrier CDMA](/wiki/Multi-carrier_code-division_multiple_access "Multi-carrier code-division multiple access") (MC-CDMA) are gaining more importance for the next generation systems. These are based on efficient [FFT](/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform "Fast Fourier transform") algorithms and frequency domain equalization, resulting in a lower number of multiplications per second. They also make it possible to control the bandwidth and form the spectrum in a flexible way. However, they require advanced dynamic channel allocation and adaptive traffic scheduling.

[WiMax](/wiki/WiMax "WiMax") is using OFDMA in the downlink and in the uplink. For the [LTE (telecommunication)](/wiki/LTE_(telecommunication) "LTE (telecommunication)"), OFDMA is used for the downlink; by contrast, [Single-carrier FDMA](/wiki/Single-carrier_FDMA "Single-carrier FDMA") is used for the uplink since OFDMA contributes more to the [PAPR](/wiki/Crest_factor "Crest factor") related issues and results in nonlinear operation of amplifiers. IFDMA provides less power fluctuation and thus requires energy-inefficient linear amplifiers. Similarly, MC-CDMA is in the proposal for the [IEEE 802.20](/wiki/802.20 "802.20") standard. These access schemes offer the same efficiencies as older technologies like CDMA. Apart from this, scalability and higher data rates can be achieved.

The other important advantage of the above-mentioned access techniques is that they require less complexity for equalization at the receiver. This is an added advantage especially in the [MIMO](/wiki/MIMO "MIMO") environments since the [spatial multiplexing](/wiki/Spatial_multiplexing "Spatial multiplexing") transmission of MIMO systems inherently require high complexity equalization at the receiver.

In addition to improvements in these multiplexing systems, improved [modulation](/wiki/Modulation "Modulation") techniques are being used. Whereas earlier standards largely used [Phase-shift keying](/wiki/Phase-shift_keying "Phase-shift keying"), more efficient systems such as 64[QAM](/wiki/QAM "QAM") are being proposed for use with the [3GPP Long Term Evolution](/wiki/3GPP_Long_Term_Evolution "3GPP Long Term Evolution") standards.

### IPv6 support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: IPv6 support")]

Unlike 3G, which is based on two parallel infrastructures consisting of [circuit switched](/wiki/Circuit_switched "Circuit switched") and [packet switched](/wiki/Packet_switched "Packet switched") network nodes, 4G is based on packet switching *only*. This requires [low-latency](/wiki/Network_latency "Network latency") data transmission.

As IPv4 addresses are (nearly) [exhausted](/wiki/IPv4_address_exhaustion "IPv4 address exhaustion"),[[Note 1]](#cite_note-37) [IPv6](/wiki/IPv6 "IPv6") is essential to support the large number of wireless-enabled devices that communicate using IP. By increasing the number of [IP addresses](/wiki/IP_address "IP address") available, IPv6 removes the need for [network address translation](/wiki/Network_address_translation "Network address translation") (NAT), a method of sharing a limited number of addresses among a larger group of devices, which has [a number of problems and limitations](/wiki/Network_address_translation#Issues_and_limitations "Network address translation"). When using IPv6, some kind of NAT is still required for communication with legacy IPv4 devices that are not also IPv6-connected.

As of June 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit), [Verizon](/wiki/Verizon_Communications "Verizon Communications") has posted specifications that require any 4G devices on its network to support IPv6.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### Advanced antenna systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Advanced antenna systems")]

Main articles: [MIMO](/wiki/MIMO "MIMO") and [Multi-user MIMO](/wiki/Multi-user_MIMO "Multi-user MIMO")

The performance of radio communications depends on an antenna system, termed [smart](/wiki/Smart_antenna "Smart antenna") or [intelligent antenna](/wiki/Intelligent_antenna "Intelligent antenna"). Recently, [multiple antenna technologies](/wiki/Multiple_antenna_research "Multiple antenna research") are emerging to achieve the goal of 4G systems such as high rate, high reliability, and long range communications. In the early 1990s, to cater for the growing data rate needs of data communication, many transmission schemes were proposed. One technology, [spatial multiplexing](/wiki/Spatial_multiplexing "Spatial multiplexing"), gained importance for its bandwidth conservation and power efficiency. Spatial multiplexing involves deploying multiple antennas at the transmitter and at the receiver. Independent streams can then be transmitted simultaneously from all the antennas. This technology, called [MIMO](/wiki/MIMO "MIMO") (as a branch of [intelligent antenna](/wiki/Intelligent_antenna "Intelligent antenna")), multiplies the base data rate by (the smaller of) the number of transmit antennas or the number of receive antennas. Apart from this, the reliability in transmitting high speed data in the fading channel can be improved by using more antennas at the transmitter or at the receiver. This is called *transmit* or *receive diversity*. Both transmit/receive diversity and transmit spatial multiplexing are categorized into the space-time coding techniques, which does not necessarily require the channel knowledge at the transmitter. The other category is closed-loop multiple antenna technologies, which require channel knowledge at the transmitter.

### Open-wireless Architecture and Software-defined radio (SDR)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Open-wireless Architecture and Software-defined radio (SDR)")]

One of the key technologies for 4G and beyond is called Open Wireless Architecture (OWA), supporting multiple wireless air interfaces in an [open architecture](/wiki/Open_architecture "Open architecture") platform.

[SDR](/wiki/Software-defined_radio "Software-defined radio") is one form of open wireless architecture (OWA). Since 4G is a collection of wireless standards, the final form of a 4G device will constitute various standards. This can be efficiently realized using SDR technology, which is categorized to the area of the radio convergence.

## History of 4G and pre-4G technologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: History of 4G and pre-4G technologies")]

In 1991, [WiLAN](/wiki/WiLAN "WiLAN") founders Hatim Zaghloul and Michel Fattouche invented [wideband](/wiki/Wideband "Wideband") [orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing](/wiki/Orthogonal_frequency-division_multiplexing "Orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing") (WOFDM), the basis for wideband [wireless communication](/wiki/Wireless_communication "Wireless communication") applications,[[39]](#cite_note-40) including 4G mobile communications.[[40]](#cite_note-ergen-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Cellular_network_standards_and_generation_timeline.svg/500px-Cellular_network_standards_and_generation_timeline.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cellular_network_standards_and_generation_timeline.svg)

Cellular network standards and generation timeline.

The 4G system was originally envisioned by the [DARPA](/wiki/DARPA "DARPA"), the US Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] DARPA selected the distributed architecture and end-to-end Internet protocol (IP), and believed at an early stage in peer-to-peer networking in which every mobile device would be both a transceiver and a router for other devices in the network, eliminating the spoke-and-hub weakness of 2G and 3G cellular systems.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Since the 2.5G GPRS system, cellular systems have provided dual infrastructures: packet switched nodes for data services, and circuit switched nodes for voice calls. In 4G systems, the circuit-switched infrastructure is abandoned and only a [packet-switched network](/wiki/Packet-switched_network "Packet-switched network") is provided, while 2.5G and 3G systems require both packet-switched and circuit-switched [network nodes](/wiki/Network_node "Network node"), i.e. two infrastructures in parallel. This means that in 4G traditional voice calls are replaced by IP telephony.

* In 2002, the strategic vision for 4G—which [ITU](/wiki/ITU "ITU") designated as [IMT Advanced](/wiki/IMT_Advanced "IMT Advanced")—was laid out.
* In 2004, [LTE](/wiki/LTE_(telecommunication) "LTE (telecommunication)") was first proposed by [NTT DoCoMo](/wiki/NTT_DoCoMo "NTT DoCoMo") of Japan.[[42]](#cite_note-43)
* In 2005, [OFDMA](/wiki/OFDMA "OFDMA") transmission technology is chosen as candidate for the [HSOPA](/wiki/HSOPA "HSOPA") downlink, later renamed 3GPP Long Term Evolution (LTE) air interface [E-UTRA](/wiki/E-UTRA "E-UTRA").
* In November 2005, [KT Corporation](/wiki/KT_(telecommunication_company) "KT (telecommunication company)") demonstrated mobile WiMAX service in [Busan](/wiki/Busan "Busan"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea").[[43]](#cite_note-kt_demo-44)
* In April 2006, [KT Corporation](/wiki/KT_Corporation "KT Corporation") started the world's first commercial mobile WiMAX service in Seoul, [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea").[[44]](#cite_note-45)
* In mid-2006, [Sprint](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation") announced that it would invest about US$5 billion in a [WiMAX](/wiki/WiMAX "WiMAX") technology buildout over the next few years[[45]](#cite_note-sprint-46) ($7.99 billion in [real](/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value_(economics) "Real versus nominal value (economics)") terms[[46]](#cite_note-inflation-US-47)). Since that time Sprint has faced many setbacks that have resulted in steep quarterly losses. On 7 May 2008, [Sprint](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation"), [Imagine](/wiki/Imagine_Communications "Imagine Communications"), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel"), [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"), [Bright House](/wiki/Bright_House_Networks "Bright House Networks"), and [Time Warner](/wiki/Time_Warner "Time Warner") announced a pooling of an average of 120 MHz of spectrum; Sprint merged its [Xohm](/wiki/Xohm "Xohm") WiMAX division with [Clearwire](/wiki/Clearwire "Clearwire") to form a company which will take the name "Clear".
* In February 2007, the [Japanese company](/wiki/Japanese_company "Japanese company") [NTT DoCoMo](/wiki/NTT_DoCoMo "NTT DoCoMo") tested a 4G communication system prototype with 4×4 [MIMO](/wiki/MIMO "MIMO") called [VSF-OFCDM](/wiki/VSF-OFCDM "VSF-OFCDM") at 100 [Mbit](/wiki/Mbit "Mbit")/s while moving, and 1 [Gbit](/wiki/Gbit "Gbit")/s while stationary. NTT DoCoMo completed a trial in which they reached a maximum packet transmission rate of approximately 5 Gbit/s in the downlink with 12×12 MIMO using a 100 MHz frequency bandwidth while moving at 10 km/h,[[47]](#cite_note-48) and is planning on releasing the first commercial network in 2010.
* In September 2007, NTT Docomo demonstrated e-UTRA data rates of 200 Mbit/s with power consumption below 100 mW during the test.[[48]](#cite_note-49)
* In January 2008, a U.S. [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (FCC) [spectrum auction](/wiki/Spectrum_auction "Spectrum auction") for the 700 MHz former analog TV frequencies began. As a result, the biggest share of the spectrum went to Verizon Wireless and the next biggest to AT&T.[[49]](#cite_note-50) Both of these companies have stated their intention of supporting [LTE](/wiki/3GPP_Long_Term_Evolution "3GPP Long Term Evolution").
* In January 2008, EU commissioner [Viviane Reding](/wiki/Viviane_Reding "Viviane Reding") suggested re-allocation of 500–800 MHz spectrum for wireless communication, including WiMAX.[[50]](#cite_note-51)
* On 15 February 2008, Skyworks Solutions released a front-end module for e-UTRAN.[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54)
* In November 2008, [ITU-R](/wiki/ITU-R "ITU-R") established the detailed performance requirements of IMT-Advanced, by issuing a Circular Letter calling for candidate Radio Access Technologies (RATs) for IMT-Advanced.[[54]](#cite_note-55)
* In April 2008, just after receiving the circular letter, the 3GPP organized a workshop on IMT-Advanced where it was decided that LTE Advanced, an evolution of current LTE standard, will meet or even exceed IMT-Advanced requirements following the ITU-R agenda.
* In April 2008, LG and Nortel demonstrated e-UTRA data rates of 50 Mbit/s while travelling at 110 km/h.[[55]](#cite_note-56)
* On 12 November 2008, [HTC](/wiki/High_Tech_Computer "High Tech Computer") announced the first WiMAX-enabled mobile phone, the [Max 4G](/wiki/Max_4G "Max 4G")[[56]](#cite_note-57)
* On 15 December 2008, [San Miguel Corporation](/wiki/San_Miguel_Corporation "San Miguel Corporation"), the largest food and beverage conglomerate in southeast Asia, has signed a memorandum of understanding with Qatar Telecom QSC ([Qtel](/wiki/Qtel "Qtel")) to build wireless broadband and mobile communications projects in the Philippines. The joint-venture formed wi-tribe Philippines, which offers 4G in the country.[[57]](#cite_note-58) Around the same time [Globe Telecom](/wiki/Globe_Telecom "Globe Telecom") rolled out the first WiMAX service in the Philippines.
* On 3 March 2009, Lithuania's LRTC announcing the first operational "4G" [mobile WiMAX](/wiki/Mobile_WiMAX "Mobile WiMAX") network in Baltic states.[[58]](#cite_note-59)
* In December 2009, Sprint began advertising "4G" service in selected cities in the United States, despite average download speeds of only 3–6 Mbit/s with peak speeds of 10 Mbit/s (not available in all markets).[[59]](#cite_note-sprint4g-60)
* On 14 December 2009, the first commercial LTE deployment was in the Scandinavian capitals [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") and [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") by the Swedish-Finnish network operator [TeliaSonera](/wiki/TeliaSonera "TeliaSonera") and its Norwegian brandname [NetCom (Norway)](/wiki/NetCom_(Norway) "NetCom (Norway)"). TeliaSonera branded the network "4G". The modem devices on offer were manufactured by [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") (dongle GT-B3710), and the network infrastructure created by [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei") (in Oslo) and [Ericsson](/wiki/Ericsson "Ericsson") (in Stockholm). TeliaSonera plans to roll out nationwide LTE across Sweden, Norway and Finland.[[60]](#cite_note-Wallstreet-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62) TeliaSonera used spectral bandwidth of 10 MHz, and single-in-single-out, which should provide physical layer [net bit rates](/wiki/Net_bit_rate "Net bit rate") of up to 50 Mbit/s downlink and 25 Mbit/s in the uplink. Introductory tests showed a [TCP](/wiki/Transmission_Control_Protocol "Transmission Control Protocol") [throughput](/wiki/Throughput "Throughput") of 42.8 Mbit/s downlink and 5.3 Mbit/s uplink in Stockholm.[[62]](#cite_note-dailymobile-63)
* On 4 June 2010, [Sprint](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation") released the first WiMAX smartphone in the US, the [HTC Evo 4G](/wiki/HTC_Evo_4G "HTC Evo 4G").[[63]](#cite_note-64)
* On November 4, 2010, the [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") Craft offered by [MetroPCS](/wiki/MetroPCS "MetroPCS") is the first commercially available LTE smartphone[[64]](#cite_note-65)
* On 6 December 2010, at the ITU World Radiocommunication Seminar 2010, the [ITU](/wiki/ITU "ITU") stated that [LTE](/wiki/3GPP_Long_Term_Evolution "3GPP Long Term Evolution"), [WiMAX](/wiki/WiMAX "WiMAX") and similar "evolved 3G technologies" could be considered "4G".[[65]](#cite_note-ITUSeminar-66)
* In 2011, [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina")'s [Claro](/wiki/Claro_Argentina,_Paraguay_and_Uruguay "Claro Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay") launched a pre-4G HSPA+ network in the country.
* In 2011, [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand")'s [Truemove-H](/wiki/True_Corporation "True Corporation") launched a pre-4G HSPA+ network with nationwide availability.
* On March 17, 2011, the [HTC Thunderbolt](/wiki/HTC_Thunderbolt "HTC Thunderbolt") offered by Verizon in the U.S. was the second LTE smartphone to be sold commercially.[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68)
* In February 2012, [Ericsson](/wiki/Ericsson "Ericsson") demonstrated [mobile-TV](https://androroot.net/mobdro-tv-latest-version-download/) over LTE, utilizing the new eMBMS service (enhanced [Multimedia Broadcast Multicast Service](/wiki/MBMS "MBMS")).[[68]](#cite_note-69)

Since 2009, the LTE-Standard has strongly evolved over the years, resulting in many deployments by various operators across the globe. For an overview of commercial LTE networks and their respective historic development see: [List of LTE networks](/wiki/List_of_LTE_networks "List of LTE networks"). Among the vast range of deployments, many operators are considering the deployment and operation of LTE networks. A compilation of planned LTE deployments can be found at: List of planned LTE networks.

## Disadvantages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Disadvantages")]

4G introduces a potential inconvenience for those who travel internationally or wish to switch carriers. In order to make and receive 4G voice calls ([VoLTE](/wiki/VoLTE "VoLTE")), the subscriber handset must not only have a matching [frequency band](/wiki/LTE_frequency_bands "LTE frequency bands") (and in some cases require [unlocking](/wiki/SIM_lock "SIM lock")), it must also have the matching enablement settings for the local carrier and/or country. While a phone purchased from a given carrier can be expected to work with that carrier, making 4G voice calls on another carrier's network (including international roaming) may be impossible without a software update specific to the local carrier and the phone model in question, which may or may not be available (although fallback to 2G/3G for voice calling may still be possible if a 2G/3G network is available with a matching frequency band).[[69]](#cite_note-VOLTE-70)

## Beyond 4G research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Beyond 4G research")]

Main article: [5G](/wiki/5G "5G")

A major issue in 4G systems is to make the high bit rates available in a larger portion of the cell, especially to users in an exposed position in between several base stations. In current research, this issue is addressed by [macro-diversity](/wiki/Macro-diversity "Macro-diversity") techniques, also known as [group cooperative relay](/wiki/Cooperative_diversity "Cooperative diversity"), and also by Beam-Division Multiple Access (BDMA).[[70]](#cite_note-71)

[Pervasive networks](/wiki/Pervasive_network "Pervasive network") are an amorphous and at present entirely hypothetical concept where the user can be simultaneously connected to several wireless access technologies and can seamlessly move between them (See [vertical handoff](/wiki/Vertical_handoff "Vertical handoff"), [IEEE 802.21](/wiki/IEEE_802.21 "IEEE 802.21")). These access technologies can be [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi"), [UMTS](/wiki/Universal_Mobile_Telecommunications_System "Universal Mobile Telecommunications System"), [EDGE](/wiki/Enhanced_Data_Rates_for_GSM_Evolution "Enhanced Data Rates for GSM Evolution"), or any other future access technology. Included in this concept is also smart-radio (also known as [cognitive radio](/wiki/Cognitive_radio "Cognitive radio")) technology to efficiently manage spectrum use and transmission power as well as the use of [mesh routing](/wiki/Mesh_networking "Mesh networking") protocols to create a pervasive network.

## The future of 4G

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4G&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: The future of 4G")]

As of 2023, many countries and regions have started the transition from 4G to 5G, the next generation of cellular technology. 5G promises even faster speeds, lower latency, and the ability to connect a vast number of devices simultaneously.

4G networks are expected to coexist with 5G networks for several years, providing coverage in areas where 5G is not available.
